headline: Shohei Ohtani Goes First Overall to North Correa, and Abbotsford-Colby Twins Stealing George Springer at Pick 53 Was the Draft's Best Move
subheadline: From Ohtani going first overall to Blake Snell falling 74 picks, the 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft had more drama than a season finale — and someone is going to regret Round 4 for the next six months.
body:
# 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft Review
### *By Bill Simmons, The Ringer*

---

## **I. The War Room Opens**

You know that scene in *The Wire* where Stringer Bell is running his drug operation like a business school case study, and you're watching thinking, "this is either genius or it's going to collapse spectacularly"? That's every fantasy baseball draft. Fourteen people sitting at their laptops or phones on March 22nd, each one convinced they've cracked the code, each one with a color-coded spreadsheet, each one about to make at least one decision that will haunt them until October. The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft was no different — except this one had *layers*. We had the reigning champion defending their throne, a two-time winner trying to reclaim relevance, and at least three managers who drafted like they were operating on four hours of sleep and a grudge.

Here's what you need to know upfront: the steals were *historically good* this year. **Blake Snell** falling to Round 13 (SEVENTY-FOUR picks below his ADP, folks — that's not a slip, that's a full-on cliff dive) is the kind of thing you frame and put on your wall. **Pete Alonso** sliding to Round 3 when his ADP said Round 2 is the sort of quiet value that wins categories in August when nobody's paying attention. Meanwhile, the reaches were equally dramatic — one manager took **George Springer** in Round 4 when the market had him going in Round 7, and another drafted **Brandon Nimmo** 38 picks early, which is the fantasy equivalent of buying a timeshare. You heard it here first.

The overall vibe of this draft? Chaotic neutral. The pitching runs came early and weird. The closer market got raided in unusual spots. And someone — I won't say who yet, but you'll know soon — drafted like they were trying to recreate a championship roster from memory instead of looking at current data. Buckle up. This is going to be fun.

---

## **II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

Let's talk about the first round, because it set the tone for everything that followed. **North Correa** held the first overall pick and did exactly what you do with the first overall pick in 2026: they took **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** in the Util slot. No drama, no controversy, no "wait, did they really just..." moment. It's Ohtani. The man hit .310 with 44 home runs and 130 RBI last season while playing for the *Los Angeles Dodgers* and frankly making everyone else feel inadequate about their lives. From there, **Athletic Park Dragons** grabbed **Aaron Judge** at two — still a monster, still the most feared right-handed bat in the game — and **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** scooped **Juan Soto** at three, which, look, Soto on the Mets with that lineup around him is a terrifying fantasy asset.

The middle of the first round got interesting. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** (your reigning champions, don't forget) took **José Ramírez** at four, which is the pick of a team that knows exactly what they're doing. **BaseballPro77** grabbed **Bobby Witt Jr.** at five — the best shortstop in baseball not named Gunnar Henderson, and even that's debatable. **Albuquerque Bombers** took **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at six coming off his torn ACL recovery, which is either a bold "he's healthy and dangerous" move or a "please don't re-injure yourself" prayer. **Hiroshima Radiation** broke the hitter run by taking **Tarik Skubal** at seven (the AL Cy Young winner goes here, fine, totally reasonable), **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** doubled down on pitching with **Paul Skenes** at eight (generational arm, zero concerns), and then the rest of the round filled out smoothly: **Elly De La Cruz**, **Kyle Tucker**, **Garrett Crochet**, **Jackson Chourio**, **Gunnar Henderson**, and **Julio Rodríguez** to close it out. Solid round top to bottom. Nobody embarrassed themselves. That comes later.

---

## **III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa** | *Draft Slot #1* | **Grade: B**

You start with Ohtani (batter version), which is inarguably correct. Then in Round 2, you take **Mookie Betts** eleven picks before his ADP, which — okay, I get it, Dodger fan energy, but Mookie at his age and injury history is a luxury, not a necessity. Round 3 brings **Chris Sale** ten picks early; Sale is one elbow twitch away from a DNF situation at any moment (we've *seen* this movie, it ends with Tommy John). The **Jack Flaherty** reach in Round 10 continues the concerning pattern. To the team's credit, **Jeff Hoffman** in Round 13 at 21 picks below ADP is a smart late-round reliever grab. But the overall picture here is a team that keeps reaching for names rather than value. They went 14th and 12th the past two seasons — this roster has upside, but the middle rounds feel like panic buying at a clearance sale.

---

**Athletic Park Dragons** | *Draft Slot #2* | **Grade: C+**

**Aaron Judge** at two is correct. Everything after that is... fine? **Cristopher Sánchez** in Round 2 is solid SP depth. **James Wood** in Round 3 is a legitimate breakout candidate. But then Rounds 9, 11, and 12 bring **Willson Contreras**, **Dansby Swanson**, and **Gleyber Torres** all taken *above* ADP — three reaches in a row in the middle rounds, which is like pulling into a gas station and getting a flat tire three times. The Dragons finished 14th last year after a 6th-place finish the year before. This draft doesn't scream "bounceback." It whispers "we hope Swanson remembers how to hit."

---

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | *Draft Slot #3* | **Grade: B+**

**Juan Soto** at three is elite value. **Bryan Woo** in Round 2 is a sneaky-good arm. **Trea Turner** in Round 3 adds speed and average. The **Mason Miller** closer pick in Round 4 is crucial in a league that scores NSVH. **Taylor Ward** as a steal in Round 11 (fifteen picks below ADP) is the kind of late-round add that wins leagues. The **Brandon Woodruff** reach in Round 9 is the only real head-scratcher — Woodruff is a question mark coming back from injury, and 18 picks early feels aggressive. But **Matthew Boyd** in Round 13 as a deep SP stash? Fine. Wisconsin Rapids finished 10th last year in their first season of recorded data; this feels like a genuine step up.

---

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | *Draft Slot #4* | **Grade: A-**

The defending champions drafting like defending champions. **José Ramírez** at four is a no-brainer anchor. **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 2 gives them elite speed and power upside. **Yordan Alvarez** in Round 3 is — hold on, Yordan in Round 3?! — that's either a steal or a testament to how deep this draft class was at the top. The **George Springer** reach in Round 4 (taken 36 picks early, a category-leading reach) is the one blemish here; Springer is 36 years old and hasn't stayed healthy in years. **Brandon Nimmo** at 38 picks early in Round 9 continues the "overpaying for aging veterans" theme. But the **Blake Snell** steal in Round 13 — 74 picks below ADP, genuinely one of the greatest late-round pickups in recent league memory — puts this draft over the top. If Snell is healthy, that's a Round 2 talent for Round 13 cost. The champs know what they're doing.

---

**BaseballPro77** | *Draft Slot #5* | **Grade: A**

Two-time champion energy radiates from every pick here. **Bobby Witt Jr.** at five is a foundational piece — .332 average, 33 homers, 43 steals last season. **Ketel Marte** in Round 2 is automatic. **Logan Gilbert** and **George Kirby** in Rounds 3 and 4 give them a Mariners pitching pipeline that's genuinely scary. **Austin Riley** in Round 5 for power. **Vinnie Pasquantino** in Round 6 for average and RBI. The **Luis Castillo** reach in Round 7 (29 picks early) is the only notable overpay. And the late rounds have depth — **Ranger Suárez**, **Gavin Williams**, **Brayan Bello**. The Mike Trout and Giancarlo Stanton picks in Rounds 13-14 are either lottery tickets or tombstones, but at that point in the draft you're just throwing darts. BaseballPro77 has won this league twice. They draft with intention. This is a championship roster.

---

**Albuquerque Bombers** | *Draft Slot #6* | **Grade: C**

**Ronald Acuña Jr.** is the centerpiece and the question mark simultaneously — if healthy, he's a first-round talent; if his knee acts up again, the whole roster shakes. **Francisco Lindor** in Round 2 is excellent. **Freddie Freeman** in Round 4 adds a high-average, high-OBP anchor. But Albuquerque has a *reach problem* that is frankly alarming. **Christian Yelich** 24 picks early, **Zack Wheeler** 32 picks early, **Sandy Alcantara** 38 picks early (Sandy Alcantara, who threw 47 innings last year, taken 38 picks above ADP — I just need a moment), and **Carlos Rodón** 24 picks early in Round 12. That's four reaches of 24+ picks. They finished 3rd last year, which was the high point in a 5-10-3 trajectory. This feels like a regression year unless Acuña is truly back.

---

**Hiroshima Radiation** | *Draft Slot #7* | **Grade: B+**

**Tarik Skubal** at seven is arguably the best pitcher in baseball right now — 2.99 ERA, 0.98 WHIP, 228 strikeouts last season. Smart anchor. **Kyle Schwarber** in Round 2 for power and OBP. **Bryce Harper** in Round 3 for everything. **Roman Anthony** in Round 4 is the best prospect sleeper in this entire draft — keep that name circled. The **Shohei Ohtani (Pitcher)** pick in Round 8 is fascinating and bold — if Ohtani pitches regularly, that's obscene value. **Nathan Eovaldi** 16 picks early in Round 9 is the only notable overpay. The Radiation finished 5th last year after an 11th — they're trending up, and this pitching-heavy approach fits their aggressive style.

---

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | *Draft Slot #8* | **Grade: A-**

The Wonderdogs have finished 4th, 5th, and 2nd the last three years. They are perpetually good and perpetually *just* missing. This draft might change that. **Paul Skenes** at eight is a generational arm — his debut numbers last year were genuinely absurd (1.96 ERA in 133 innings). **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** in Round 2 provides elite SP depth. **Pete Alonso** as a steal in Round 3 (14 picks below ADP, the man hit 41 homers last year) is *outstanding* value. **Josh Hader** falling 12 picks in Round 10 gives them a closer. **William Contreras** at catcher is rock solid. The **Geraldo Perdomo** pick in Round 5 is a bit puzzling for the categories this league scores. But overall? This is a well-constructed roster. The Wonderdogs are dangerous.

---

**Buttville Skidmarks** | *Draft Slot #9* | **Grade: B-**

Look, **Elly De La Cruz** is a fantasy monster — .259 average, 25 homers, *67 steals* last season. That's the pick of the draft if we're just talking raw upside at position 9. **Junior Caminero** in Round 2 is an exciting power bat. **Wyatt Langford** in Round 3 and **Rafael Devers** in Round 4 add solid thump. But the pitching here is shaky — **Kyle Bradish** is coming off Tommy John, **Kevin Gausman** is fine but not elite, and **MacKenzie Gore** taken 17 picks early in Round 10 is a gamble on a guy who's been inconsistent. **Spencer Strider** in Round 11 as a late-round flier? Bold. **Bryan Reynolds** 21 picks early in Round 12 is a mild reach. The Skidmarks have hovered around 8th for three years. The offense is there; pitching could sink them.

---

**General Hospital** | *Draft Slot #10* | **Grade: C-**

Back-to-back 13th-place finishes. Let that sink in. And then they used their Round 8 pick on **Luis Robert Jr.** — taken 46 PICKS ABOVE ADP. Forty-six. Robert has played more than 100 games exactly once in his career. Picking him 46 spots early is the fantasy equivalent of betting your mortgage on a horse that's currently on the injured list. **Kyle Tucker** at ten is excellent. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** in Round 2 is great. **Manny Machado** and **Max Fried** in Rounds 3-4 are solid. But that Robert reach in Round 8 is the kind of decision that defines a draft retrospectively. Some late-round prospect gambles are intriguing too. Can General Hospital finally escape the basement? Maybe. But I'm not betting on it.

---

**Sugar Land Skeeters** | *Draft Slot #11* | **Grade: A**

The reigning champions are doing reigning champion things. **Garrett Crochet** at 11 is tremendous value — his first full season as a starter in Boston is going to be must-watch. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** in Round 2 brings elite speed-power combo. **Matt Olson** falling 12 picks below ADP in Round 4 for a guy who hit 40+ homers? *Steal*. **Edwin Díaz** dropping 20 picks in Round 6 adds elite closing stats. **Ryan Helsley** falling 14 picks in Round 11 rounds out the bullpen beautifully. The only head-scratchers are **Max Muncy** taken 30 picks early in Round 12 and **Trent Grisham** taken 18 picks early in Round 14 — but at that point you're just padding. This is a championship-caliber roster constructed by a team that knows *exactly* what the 12-category scoring system rewards.

---

**Riverside Roughnecks** | *Draft Slot #12* | **Grade: C+**

The Roughnecks have finished 9th, 9th, and 11th. Consistent mediocrity! **Jackson Chourio** at 12 is legitimate — 25 homers, 40 steals, 22 years old, the trajectory is north. **Nick Kurtz** in Round 2 is an interesting prospect bet. **Cole Ragans** taken 14 picks early in Round 3 is an overpay for a guy who has ace *stuff* but inconsistent *results*. **Trevor Story** taken 25 picks early in Round 7 is the kind of reach that makes you wince — Story has played a combined 121 games over the last three seasons. The **Carlos Estévez** steal in Round 13 (51 picks below ADP — the biggest steal by raw number in this draft) and **Kenley Jansen** falling 22 picks in Round 14 are excellent late-round bullpen finds. The Roughnecks are better than their history suggests, but the middle-round reaches are their annual Achilles heel.

---

**Canberra Wombats** | *Draft Slot #13* | **Grade: A-**

The Wombats have a reputation for finding steals, and they delivered again. **Gunnar Henderson** at 13 — arguably the best pure hitter available at that slot. **Cal Raleigh** in Round 2 is the best catcher in fantasy baseball. **Logan Webb** in Round 3 anchors the rotation. **Pete Crow-Armstrong** in Round 4 brings elite speed. And then — *and then* — they grab **Jacob deGrom** 13 picks below ADP in Round 5. Yes, deGrom is a health risk. He's also the most dominant pitcher alive when functional. **Jose Altuve** falling 27 picks in Round 9 and **Jo Adell** falling 26 picks in Round 12 add to a steal-heavy portfolio. **Aaron Nola** as a Round 15 steal completes the picture. The Wombats finished 4th last year and are trending back toward their 2023 third-place form. They might be the most dangerous team in the middle rounds.

---

**Benchwarmers** | *Draft Slot #14* | **Grade: B**

Last pick in the first round, but the Benchwarmers get the second pick in Round 2 — so **Julio Rodríguez** and **Corbin Carroll** back-to-back is genuinely exciting outfield speed-and-upside. **Zach Neto** and **Brent Rooker** in Rounds 3-4 add balance. **Cody Bellinger** falling 13 picks in Round 5 is a smart buy-low on a guy who can still hit when motivated. **Emilio Pagán** dropping 23 picks in Round 14 is a classic Benchwarmers late-round bullpen find — they've made a habit of this. **Michael King** and **Sonny Gray** give them SP depth. The Benchwarmers have bounced from 14th to 7th to 9th — this roster, with the right injury luck, gets them into the top six.

---

## **IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**The Three Best Value Picks:**

🏆 **Blake Snell, SP — Abbotsford-Colby Twins, Round 13, Pick 172 (ADP: 98, fell 74 picks)**
Seventy-four picks. That's not a steal, that's grand larceny. Snell has a career ERA under 3.50 and is now with the Dodgers. If he's healthy — and that's always the caveat with Snell — this is a Round 1 talent in Round 13. The defending champs may have just locked up their second title with this single pick.

🏆 **Pete Alonso, 1B — Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, Round 3, Pick 36 (ADP: 22, fell 14 picks)**
Alonso hit 41 home runs and drove in 108 runs last year. His ADP said Round 2. He fell into Round 3. The Wonderdogs just got a top-25 player at Round 3 cost. In a 12-category league where HR and RBI are scored, that's borderline criminal value.

🏆 **Carlos Estévez, RP — Riverside Roughnecks, Round 13, Pick 180 (ADP: 129, fell 51 picks)**
Fifty-one picks below ADP for a closer who saved 35+ games last year. The Roughnecks' late-round bullpen strategy is the only reason they might escape the 9th-place curse.

**The Three Worst Reaches:**

💀 **Brandon Nimmo, OF — Abbotsford-Colby Twins, Round 9, Pick 116 (ADP: 154, taken 38 picks early)**
Nimmo is now with the Rangers, he's 31, and he hasn't had a truly healthy full season in recent memory. Taking him 38 picks early in Round 9 when you're the defending champions and presumably don't *need* to gamble this aggressively is puzzling.

💀 **Luis Robert Jr., OF — General Hospital, Round 8, Pick 103 (ADP: 149, taken 46 picks early)**
The biggest reach in the entire draft by number of picks. Robert has the talent but not the health history to justify a 46-pick premium. For a team that's finished 13th two years running, this is exactly the kind of boom-or-bust swing that keeps them in the basement.

💀 **Sandy Alcantara, SP — Albuquerque Bombers, Round 9, Pick 118 (ADP: 156, taken 38 picks early)**
Alcantara threw 47 innings in 2025 returning from Tommy John surgery. He's a brilliant pitcher when healthy. He has not been healthy. Taking him 38 picks early is optimistic to the point of delusion.

---

## **V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

**1. Sugar Land Skeeters repeat as champions.** The combination of **Garrett Crochet**, **Fernando Tatis Jr.**, **Matt Olson**, **Edwin Díaz**, and **Ryan Helsley** — all acquired at or below market value — gives them depth that other contenders simply can't match across 12 categories. The Skeeters have now finished 3rd, 1st — the arrow points up.

**2. General Hospital finishes 13th or 14th for the third consecutive year.** The **Luis Robert Jr.** reach in Round 8 will be the defining decision of their season. Robert will miss at least 40 games, the middle-round pitching depth is thin, and back-to-back last-place finishes have a momentum of their own.

**3. Hiroshima Radiation breaks through and wins their first title.** **Tarik Skubal** anchoring a rotation that also includes **Shohei Ohtani (Pitcher)** — if Ohtani pitches 150 innings — is the most dominant pitching setup in the league. **Roman Anthony** emerges as the breakout bat of 2026. The Radiation finished 2nd-11th-5th; the 2026 championship is their ceiling.

**4. The Blake Snell pick saves Abbotsford-Colby Twins' title defense.** The **George Springer** and **Brandon Nimmo** reaches will cost them at least 15 category wins across the season, but Snell in Round 13 provides enough SP surplus to paper over those holes. They finish top four.

**5. Canberra Wombats' **Jacob deGrom** returns to form and becomes the most talked-about pick of the entire draft.** Everyone will say they "knew" deGrom was healthy. They didn't. But if he gives them 25 starts with a sub-3.00 ERA, that Round 5 steal at Pick 69 becomes the stuff of legend.

---

## **VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

Give me the **Sugar Land Skeeters**.

Here's why: they won the championship last year, and instead of getting complacent, they drafted with *more* precision. **Garrett Crochet** at 11 is legitimately the second or third best pitcher in fantasy baseball if he stays healthy in Boston's new AL East environment. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** in Round 2 gives them the kind of five-category contributor (average, runs, homers, RBI, steals) that wins head-to-head matchups. **Matt Olson** at 34th overall ADP cost for a Round 4 pick is one of the five best value plays of the entire draft. And then — *then* — they just keep finding closers. **Edwin Díaz** (20 picks below ADP), **Ryan Helsley** (14 picks below ADP). In a league that scores NSVH as a closing category, the Skeeters are essentially printing free wins every time one of their guys logs a save.

The only thing that stops them is health. But that's true of every team. On paper, constructed with the cold-blooded efficiency of a team that has won before and knows *exactly* what it takes to win again, the Sugar Land Skeeters are your 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball champions.

See you in October. Bring beer.

*— Simmons*
